prompt: What is the correct negation of ∀x(P(x) → Q(x))?
hint: Push ¬ through the quantifier (¬∀ becomes ∃) and then negate the conditional (¬(A → B) = A ∧ ¬B).
explanation: ¬∀x(P(x) → Q(x)) becomes ∃x¬(P(x) → Q(x)). Since ¬(A → B) ≡ A ∧ ¬B, this is ∃x(P(x) ∧ ¬Q(x)) — 'some P is not Q', the counterexample. 'Not all P are Q' means exactly 'there is a P that is not Q'.
